text: Having developed the expertise of esophageal mural tunneling for the purpose of the myotomies, we elected to apply this technique to esophageal lengthening, performing a bilateral myotomy submucosally
only concerning the circular fibers of the esophageal wall, and then performing a magnetic anastomosis with a magnimosis system.
So the first step of the technique consists in creating a defect in the esophagus thoracoscopically, just cutting the esophagus with a stapler.
In the pig model, the esophagus has very, very few attachments.
So as soon as you cut it, it will just spring back.
And so it wasn't necessary to create a big gap in order to have great distance between the two esophageal stems.
Here you see the distance between the two esophageal stems, the proximal and the distal, before the myotomy.
This is a combined approach, a gastric approach, with a PEG technique and esophageal approach.
You see the endoscope in the proximal oesophageal stamp.
The myotomy has been created initially with a mucosal lift done with saline and methylene blue.
The mucosa is then incised with a monopolar needle knife.
Very low coterie setting at about 20.
And once this initial opening is performed, the needle knife is guided through the opening of the mucosa.
And it's going to serve to gently dilate.
You can also use a dilating balloon, but that is rather expensive, so we do not in the animal lab.
The scope is just driven on the guidance of this needle knife into the mucosal tunneling.
We're using very low CO2 insufflation, about 8 millimeters of mercury.
As you do, it's very useful.
We'll show you exactly where to go and we'll give you the right orientation.
It's very easy to get confused when you are in this blind space.
And here you see how nicely you can really tell the difference between the mucosa on the top that has this very particular vascular structure and the muscle layer.
What we do, we initiate the myotomy using the needle knife, very low setting again.
And then we will prolong the myotomy proximally using an IT knife, which I like because it's very delicate and has a protective ceramic tip, or the triangle knife.
It's very important at this point to only cut the circular fibers and not the longitudinal fibers.
In the past, several types of myotomy have been used to elongate the proximal stamp in the treatment of atresia.
But the problem was that the myotomy concerned both the circular and longitudinal fibers that created outpatching in the reticular long term.
And also cut off the blood supply.
with not exactly good results.

You have a bulk of metal in the esophageal lumen, which is going to be difficult for the animal to tolerate.
